metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact YAML annotation key and value type for expected unmet criteria (e.g., `unmet_criteria: ["criterion-A", "criterion-B"]` under the case entry) so the scorer and YAML spec share a documented interface.
findings.finding: The annotation schema for 'expected unmet criteria' in the eval YAML case is unspecified — the plan says the CV-two-unmet-criteria case is 'annotated with the two expected criteria' but never states the YAML key name, value type, or format (e.g., a list of strings under key `expected_unmet_criteria`). The `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer must read this annotation, so both files depend on the same interface. An executor must invent the schema, creating a real risk of scorer/YAML mismatch that `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` may not catch (it checks scorer registration, not schema conformance).
findings.location: What section / eval_scorers.py + completion-verifier.eval.yaml / AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the What section: 'The case entry in the YAML carries an `unmet_criteria` key whose value is a list of criterion-label strings (e.g., `unmet_criteria: ["missing-implementation", "untested-path"]`); the scorer reads this list and checks that the verdict contains one distinct finding per entry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the case-level annotation field name and shape (e.g. `unmet_criteria: ["AC label A", "AC label B"]`) and the matching predicate the scorer uses to determine whether a FAIL finding 'names' a given unmet criterion (e.g. case-insensitive substring of `finding['criterion']` against the annotation string), so the YAML case and scorer implementation share an unambiguous contract.
findings.finding: The `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer's required case-level annotation field — the structured data telling the scorer which specific criteria must each appear in a distinct finding — is never named or shaped in the plan, and the matching predicate (how a finding is judged to 'name' a given unmet criterion) is also unspecified. This is the core input contract of the new scorer: without it, the YAML case author and the scorer implementer cannot agree on the field name or its structure, and two conforming implementations would be mutually incompatible.
findings.location: What section — `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer description; AC line: 'given a case annotated with the set of independently-unmet criteria, it scores 1.0 iff the FAIL verdict emits a distinct finding for EACH'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Annotate the `CV-two-unmet-criteria` case with `unmet_criteria: ["<label-A>", "<label-B>"]` (a list of short criterion identifiers). The scorer reads `case.get("unmet_criteria") or []` and, for each label, checks that at least one finding's `criterion` field contains the label as a case-insensitive substring; it scores 1.0 only when every label is matched by a distinct finding.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact case-level annotation field the `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer reads (e.g. `unmet_criteria: ["AC gap 1", "AC gap 2"]`) so the YAML dataset case and the scorer implementation share an explicit contract.
findings.finding: The plan says the `CV-two-unmet-criteria` case is 'annotated with the two expected criteria' that the scorer reads, but never names the annotation field. The scorer's implementation contract (what key it reads from the case dict) is left unspecified, creating an ambiguity between the YAML author and the scorer implementer.
findings.location: What section — `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer description
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the scorer spec: 'The case annotation key is `unmet_criteria` (a list of strings); the scorer reads `case["unmet_criteria"]` and scores 1.0 iff the verdict findings contain a distinct finding for each string in the list (case-insensitive substring match on criterion/detail).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the case-level annotation field that `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` reads (e.g., `unmet_criteria: ["fn not implemented", "path not tested"]`) in both the scorer spec and the `CV-two-unmet-criteria` YAML case definition.
findings.finding: The case-level annotation field for `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` is never named. The plan says cases are 'annotated with the set of independently-unmet criteria' but does not specify the YAML field key the scorer reads (e.g., `unmet_criteria`, `expected_criteria`, or some other key). Without this, an implementer must guess the field name, and the scorer and the YAML case will be written independently with no guaranteed consistency.
findings.location: What section / AC line 2: 'annotated with the set of independently-unmet criteria'; src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py (new scorer); src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml (CV-two-unmet-criteria case)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the plan's scorer spec: 'The scorer reads `case["unmet_criteria"]` — a list of strings, one per independently-unmet criterion. It is `_NA` when the key is absent or `expect` is not `fail`.' Mirror the same field name in the YAML case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the matching predicate for `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria`: which finding field(s) are checked (e.g., `criterion` + `detail`), what match strategy is used (substring, normalized lowercase), and how 'distinct finding per criterion' is counted (one finding must match each criterion string; a finding matching two criteria counts only once).
findings.finding: The matching predicate inside `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` — what it means for a finding to 'name' a given unmet criterion — is unspecified. The plan says 'each naming a different unmet criterion' but does not say which finding field(s) are searched, what match strategy is used (substring, exact, normalized), or how duplicate/overlapping findings are counted as 'distinct'.
findings.location: What section / AC line 2: scorer description; src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py (new scorer implementation)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `_NA` / applicability condition for `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` (e.g., 'returns `_NA` when `case.get("unmet_criteria")` is absent or empty, so pass cases and unannotated fail cases are excluded from the scorer's denominator').
findings.finding: The plan does not specify when `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` returns `_NA` (not applicable). Every existing scorer has an explicit guard (e.g., `if case.get('expect') not in FIRE_EXPECTS: return _NA`). Without an applicability rule, the scorer's denominator is undefined: it either applies to every case (including `expect: pass` cases where there are no unmet criteria, making `>= N findings` unsatisfiable and always returning 0.0) or requires a guard the plan omits.
findings.location: What section / AC line 2 (scorer spec); src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py (new scorer)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC-2 to clarify that adding the yaml case without the scorer BREAKS strict validation, and that both the scorer registration and the yaml reference must land together (or scorer-first) for strict mode to pass on the extended spec.
findings.finding: The plan's AC-2 states 'Offline proof: validate_eval_spec(strict=True) passes on the extended spec (which it can only do because the scorer is registered).' This frames strict-mode validation as passing on the NEW, extended spec — but the existing completion-verifier.eval.yaml already passes validate_eval_spec(strict=True) today (all its named scorers are already in REGISTRY). The plan never probes whether the CURRENT spec passes strict validation, so it cannot confirm that adding the new scorer+case is what makes the gate pass vs. break.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC-2
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC-2's offline-proof sentence to: 'Offline proof: validate_eval_spec(strict=True) passes on the extended spec only when BOTH the scorer is registered in REGISTRY AND the yaml references it — adding the yaml case alone breaks strict validation until the scorer is registered.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the byte-unchanged constraint, e.g., `git diff HEAD -- src/rebar/llm/completion.py` showing no lines changed in `reconcile_verdict`, or a `grep`/hash check against the function.
findings.finding: AC4 ('reconcile_verdict and the close-path logic are byte-unchanged') has no stated proving command. 'Byte-unchanged' is a strong claim but without a command (e.g., `git diff HEAD -- src/rebar/llm/completion.py | grep reconcile_verdict` or a checksum assertion) there is no in-session way to verify the constraint was upheld.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / AC4

findings.finding: AC4 states 'reconcile_verdict and the close-path logic are byte-unchanged' — a concrete, specific claim — but provides no proving command (e.g., a `git diff` assertion or hash check) to verify it. Without a command, a reviewer cannot distinguish 'unchanged' from 'accidentally touched during refactoring.'
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: 'reconcile_verdict and the close-path logic are byte-unchanged (no reconciliation/threshold edits).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'Verified by `git diff HEAD -- src/rebar/llm/completion/reconcile_verdict.py` (and the close-path module) producing no output in the final PR.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add unit tests for `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer logic (e.g., case with 0 findings → 0.0, case with 1 of 2 expected criteria → 0.0, case with repeated same criterion → 0.0, case with PASS verdict not FAIL → 0.0, and the happy-path 2-of-2 → 1.0) so that `make test` exercises the scorer's branching logic, not just its registration.
findings.finding: The new `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer introduces conditional branching logic (score 1.0 vs 0.0 based on finding count and per-criterion distinctness), but the plan specifies no unit tests for the scorer's own logic. The only in-suite gate is `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)`, which verifies registration only — not behavioral correctness. `make test` can pass with a completely incorrect scorer implementation (e.g., always returns 1.0), because the actual scoring logic is only exercised in the external/[eval] CI run.
findings.location: AC: enumerates_all_unmet_criteria scorer (evals/eval_scorers.py) + 'make test passes on the change'
findings.suggested_fix: In tests/llm/evals/test_eval_scorers.py (or equivalent), add parametrized unit tests for `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` covering: (a) empty findings list → 0.0, (b) one finding naming criterion A when both A and B expected → 0.0, (c) two findings both naming criterion A → 0.0, (d) two findings naming A and B respectively → 1.0, (e) verdict is PASS not FAIL → 0.0 (if scorer checks verdict type).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `test_op_fail_findings_each_have_criterion_and_detail` (test_completion_verifier.py:371–401) already covers the proposed 'multi-finding FAIL survives reconcile_verdict' invariant; if so, extend that test rather than adding a duplicate.
findings.finding: The proposed FakeRunner unit test ('asserting a two-finding FAIL verdict is preserved (all findings intact) through reconcile_verdict + the close path') substantially duplicates existing test coverage. `test_op_fail_findings_each_have_criterion_and_detail` (tests/interfaces/store/test_completion_verifier.py, lines 371–401) already seeds a 2-finding FAIL verdict through FakeRunner → normalize → resolve_citations → reconcile_verdict → validate and asserts both findings survive with criterion + detail intact. Proposing a near-identical test without first checking whether the existing one already covers the invariant is a Rule-of-Three / NIH anti-pattern at the test level.
findings.location: What / 'Additionally add a FakeRunner unit test … asserting the PLUMBING invariant: a multi-finding FAIL verdict survives reconcile_verdict and the close path with all findings intact'; AC line 'A FakeRunner unit test asserts a two-finding FAIL verdict is preserved (all findings intact) through reconcile_verdict + the close path.'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `test_op_fail_findings_each_have_criterion_and_detail` with an assertion that `len(r['findings']) == 2` (not just `>= 1`) and that both findings' `criterion` values are distinct — this is the one gap the existing test leaves, and it fits naturally in the test that already exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `tests/unit/test_new_eval_specs.py` as a consumer that must remain green: it runs `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` on `completion-verifier` and checks every deterministic scorer name is in `REGISTRY`, so the YAML edit and the `eval_scorers.py` registration must land in the same atomic change.
findings.finding: `test_new_eval_specs.py` is an unenumerated consumer that runs `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` over `completion-verifier` and checks every deterministic scorer name is registered. Adding `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` to the YAML before it is also in `REGISTRY` would break this existing offline test, and the plan does not name it as a file to be aware of.
findings.location: What section / AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Note: `tests/unit/test_new_eval_specs.py` already runs `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` on `completion-verifier` — the YAML and `REGISTRY` edits must be committed together to keep this test green.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the `completion_verifier.md` front-matter `description` to reflect the anti-ratchet requirement (e.g., 'Emits a PASS/FAIL verdict with one finding per EACH failing criterion — all unmet criteria enumerated in a single verdict') so the index.json summary stays consistent with the new prompt body.
findings.finding: The `completion_verifier.md` front-matter `description` field still reads 'Emits a PASS/FAIL verdict with **one finding per failing criterion**' — this will contradict the new prompt section once the plan's 'exhaustive evaluation / one finding per unmet criterion' requirement is added. A reader of the index.json (which renders the description) will see a stale summary that downplays the anti-ratchet requirement.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/reviewers/completion_verifier.md lines 4–8 (front-matter description field); plan 'What' section bullet 1
findings.suggested_fix: Change the description field to: 'Verifies that a ticket's completion requirements are demonstrably met before closure. On FAIL, enumerates ALL unmet criteria — one finding per criterion — in a single verdict (anti-ratchet: a partial verdict naming only a subset of failures is invalid).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the eval-run pass policy for AC2: e.g., 'a single passing eval run suffices' or 'scorer must be 1.0 on at least 2 of 3 runs' so the executor knows when the criterion is closed.
findings.finding: AC2's behavioral proof (scorer == 1.0 on the eval run) depends on a live LLM call, which is inherently stochastic, yet the plan treats it as a deterministic binary gate with no stated pass policy (single run, majority of N runs, fixed seed, etc.). An executor cannot reliably close this AC without knowing whether one passing run suffices or whether retries/averaging are expected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / AC2 / 'Behavioral proof: the eval run ... shows ... scorer == 1.0'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'A single eval-run result of scorer == 1.0 for CV-two-unmet-criteria is sufficient to close this criterion; if the run scores 0.0, treat it as a prompt defect requiring investigation (not a retry).`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground the FakeRunner-limitation claim by reading `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` (or the equivalent FakeRunner source) and adding a one-line citation (e.g. 'FakeRunner.run returns `self._structured` verbatim without executing the prompt — see runner.py line N') to the plan's rationale for requiring an eval case.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'a FakeRunner test only returns pre-loaded findings, so it cannot demonstrate the prompt achieves exhaustive evaluation' as a factual claim about FakeRunner's capabilities, but the plan never grounds this in a read of the FakeRunner implementation. If FakeRunner had some evaluation capability, the rationale for requiring an eval case (rather than a FakeRunner test) would be undermined.
findings.location: What section, paragraph beginning 'Prove the behavior with an EVAL case, not a tautological FakeRunner test'

findings.finding: The plan's AC2 requires adding a deterministic scorer named `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` to `completion-verifier.eval.yaml` AND states that `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` passes on the extended spec — but `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` rejects any deterministic scorer not present in `REGISTRY` in `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py` (confirmed: eval.py:311-317 loads `known_scorer_names()` and `validate_scorer` rejects unlisted names; `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` is absent from `REGISTRY`). The plan never mentions adding the scorer implementation to `eval_scorers.py`, so the stated AC2 self-consistency claim ('validate_eval_spec(strict=True) passes') rests on an unbacked assumption that the scorer name will be registered.
findings.location: AC2 / What section ('new deterministic scorer enumerates_all_unmet_criteria') / src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py REGISTRY
findings.suggested_fix: Add a work item: 'Add `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` to `REGISTRY` in `src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py`. The scorer applies only to `expect: fail` cases that declare `min_findings: N` (or embed N in the case metadata); it passes when `len(out["findings"]) >= N` and the N criterion fields are pairwise distinct.' Then update AC2 to reference this file explicitly.

findings.finding: The plan requires `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` to pass on the extended spec (AC item 2), but strict mode validates that every deterministic scorer name is registered in `eval_scorers.REGISTRY` (src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py). The new `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer name is not currently in REGISTRY, and `eval_scorers.py` is not named as an edit target anywhere in the plan — so adding it only to the .eval.yaml will cause `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` to fail.
findings.location: What section / AC item 2 / src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml
findings.suggested_fix: In eval_scorers.py, implement a scorer function (e.g. `_enumerates_all_unmet_criteria`) that, given a `CV-two-unmet-criteria` case, checks that the output's `findings` list contains at least two entries each naming a distinct criterion. Register it in `REGISTRY` as `'enumerates_all_unmet_criteria': _enumerates_all_unmet_criteria`.

findings.finding: The plan requires `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` to pass (AC2) but never mentions implementing the `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer in `eval_scorers.py`. Strict validation calls `known_scorer_names()` which returns `frozenset(REGISTRY)` — any deterministic scorer name absent from that registry is rejected. Adding the scorer name only to the YAML without a matching registry entry will cause strict validation to fail, breaking AC2.
findings.location: AC2 / src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py (REGISTRY) / src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/completion-verifier.eval.yaml
findings.suggested_fix: Add to eval_scorers.py: a scorer function `_enumerates_all_unmet_criteria(case, out)` that (a) is only applicable when `case.get('expect') == 'fail'` and the case carries a `min_findings` or `criteria_count` field (e.g. 2 for CV-two-unmet-criteria), (b) counts distinct `criterion` values in `_findings(out)` and returns `ScoreResult(True, count >= expected_n)`. Then register it in REGISTRY as `'enumerates_all_unmet_criteria': _enumerates_all_unmet_criteria`. Also add `criteria_count: 2` to the CV-two-unmet-criteria dataset case so the scorer knows N=2.

findings.finding: The plan adds a new deterministic scorer `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` to completion-verifier.eval.yaml and asserts AC2 ('validate_eval_spec(strict=True) passes on the extended spec'), but validate_eval_spec(strict=True) rejects any deterministic scorer name not registered in eval_scorers.REGISTRY — and the plan never mentions implementing this scorer in eval_scorers.py.
findings.location: AC2 / What section ('Add to completion-verifier.eval.yaml … enumerates_all_unmet_criteria scorer') / src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py REGISTRY
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'Register `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` in `eval_scorers.REGISTRY` (src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py): a scorer that, on a FAIL verdict, checks len(findings) >= N where N is the number of independently-unmet criteria in the dataset case (e.g. carried in a `min_findings` field per case). Without this, validate_eval_spec(strict=True) will reject the scorer name.' Also add a corresponding AC bullet.

findings.finding: The plan introduces a new deterministic scorer `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` in completion-verifier.eval.yaml but never specifies adding it to the REGISTRY in eval_scorers.py — `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` (which AC line 2 requires to pass) will reject any deterministic scorer name absent from REGISTRY.
findings.location: What section / AC line 2 / src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py REGISTRY (lines 370-401) / src/rebar/llm/evals/eval.py validate_scorer (line 129)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' bullet for the eval YAML change, add: 'Also register `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` in `eval_scorers.REGISTRY` (eval_scorers.py) — strict validation rejects deterministic scorer names absent from the registry.'

findings.finding: The plan's new `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer name is referenced only in the eval YAML, but `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` — which AC line 2 requires to pass — rejects any deterministic scorer name not present in `eval_scorers.REGISTRY`. The scorer is absent from the registry; the plan never mentions adding it there.
findings.location: What section / AC line 2: 'Offline validate_eval_spec(strict=True) passes on the extended spec'; src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_scorers.py REGISTRY
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scorer function to eval_scorers.py — e.g. `def _enumerates_all_unmet_criteria(case, out): findings = _findings(out); n = case.get('min_findings', 1); return ScoreResult(case.get('expect') in FIRE_EXPECTS, len(findings) >= n and len({f.get('criterion') for f in findings}) >= n)` — and register it under `'enumerates_all_unmet_criteria'` in REGISTRY, then reference `min_findings: 2` in the `CV-two-unmet-criteria` dataset case.

findings.finding: AC2 requires only `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` (an offline schema/format check) as the proving command for the eval case, but the central behavioral claim — that the prompt actually emits two distinct findings for two unmet criteria — can only be proven by running the live eval (e.g., `pytest -m eval` or equivalent). A passing schema-validation command does not exercise the prompt or the scorer at all, so the behavioral proof has no AC-level proving command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'Offline `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` passes on the extended spec.'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2 to: 'Offline `validate_eval_spec(strict=True)` passes on the extended spec; AND running the eval suite (`pytest --extras eval -k CV-two-unmet-criteria` or equivalent) exits 0 with the `enumerates_all_unmet_criteria` scorer reporting pass, confirming the prompt emits >= 2 distinct findings for the two-gap fixture.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
